topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, the paper yellowed at the edges, and the handwriting was familiar, yet strangely distant. Elias held it under the harsh glare of his desk lamp, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest. It had been fifteen years since the argument, fifteen years since the words had been exchanged, and now this—a formal apology—landed in his inbox. He reread the carefully chosen phrasing, his eyes scanning the familiar script, searching for a flicker of the person he remembered, but finding only a polished, almost sterile distance. He crumpled the letter once, then smoothed it out again, his jaw tight. Every muscle in his neck felt coiled, ready to snap. He pushed the chair back with a scrape that echoed too loudly in the small room, needing movement, needing to dissipate the heavy pressure building behind his ribs.

[story 2]

Clara traced the embossed seal on the stationery with a trembling finger. The afternoon light slanted across the worn wooden floor, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air, making the whole room feel suspended and fragile. She kept glancing at the door, anticipating a knock that never came, or perhaps a call that wouldn't connect. The contents of the letter sat on the kitchen counter, an unwelcome artifact from a life she had meticulously built around forgetting. She picked up the pen, hovering it over the paper, the ink seeming too heavy, too final. A slight tremor ran through her hand, causing her to set it down sharply. She walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the indifferent cityscape, feeling a profound weight settle onto her shoulders, making each breath shallow and difficult to draw in.

[story 3]

Mark stared at the letter as if it were a foreign object dropped into his carefully ordered world. The scent of aged paper and faint lavender wafted up from the envelope. He kept running his thumb over the crease in the corner, a repetitive, anxious motion. He paced the length of his living room three times, the sound of his own footsteps sharp and uneven on the hardwood. His breathing became shallow, catching in his throat whenever he looked down at the typed words. He wanted to burn it, to destroy the evidence of this long-dormant conflict, but his hand remained frozen above the paper. The tension coiled tighter, a visible rigidity in his posture, as he fought the urge to simply drop everything and retreat into the quiet darkness.
